transcript: My name is John Wesley Hakebott, name Buck. I'm at the present time 63 years old. I have three children, eight grandchildren, of whom I'm very proud. I grew up in a little town of Gary Oklahoma. Population of about 1800, if you counted all the farmers in the outlying areas and probably the farm animals. The main street of this town was approximately two blocks long. It was intersected by Broadway, which was also about two blocks long. And that was about the main business district in the town. Farming was probably the only thing that really went on in the town. My earliest remembrances is of particularly being without a father. My father died when I was about three years old, a little older, maybe. I don't remember much or anything about him other than, I remember the day he was buried, standing across the street with my brothers after the funeral, watching them load the casket in the hearse and leaving for the cemetery. My brothers and I didn't go. I also remember distinctly hearing the old rugged cross at his funeral. But only in that I don't remember anything. And I'm not sure I know whether I missed anything or not. Not having known him at all. I was the last of ten kids in the family. I came along as an afterthought. The nearest brother to me was about six years older than I am. Two of my older sisters were already married. In fact, I had a niece that's just two weeks younger than I am. The house that I remember first, not the one that I was born in, it was about three blocks away from this house. But it was a small house. I believe it had two bedrooms. Wood floors. I can remember my mom's old cook stove in the kitchen. A summer and winter that had to go on because in order to support the family, she had to take in laundry. She had no training or anything else as she was married when she was about 15 years old. And she had an extremely rough life. In those days to take in laundry had to be done by hand. One of my first jobs that I can remember was being in the backyard and having to keep the fire going under the big old cast iron pot to heat the water for her to wash with. The actual laundry, of course, was done on a scrub board by hand. And then hung out to dry. And then all the items that had to be ironed had to be done with an iron heated on the cook stove. And it had a little clamp handle that she clapped onto the iron to iron with. At one time my brother, Jay, she showed him how to iron. And she used to pay him, I think, a nickel of a piece for any ironing that he did. And also, most of this laundry came from the CCC camp. You know, that we had in the town. One of my older brothers, Basel, was in that CCC camp. We would go down once a week, my brother's and I, with a cart today had built out of bicycle wheels with a board on it and a long ton with crossmembers on that ton so that we could be on each side of the tongue and lean on these crossmembers and push on them to pull the cart. We would go down. It was about a mile away and we'd pick up the dirty clothes. We would leave the packages of clean clothes and bring them home and this one on every day for my mom. And it was a major project because in between she had to make her own soap out of lye and ashes. I don't remember exactly how it went but that man and another fire had to be built and a big, huge boiler put on the fire to make this soap. It wasn't particularly easy but I didn't know that. Life was very simple. There was no TV. I didn't go to a movie until I was probably eight or nine years old. The main thing we did was entertain ourselves. My sister Anna lived about a block and a half away from us down a little dirt road. We were also living on a dirt road. You used to spend an awful lot of time down with her and my niece's nephews there. In the summer I remember particularly my brother coming up in the evenings with a bunch of the guys from the CCC camp and he played the guitar. My sister played pump organ and I can remember sitting outside in the evening and all these guys sitting around we popped some corn or toasts and peanuts which we raised ourselves. Just sitting around enjoying singing songs, playing games out in the street to go in and have to window, drop the hangative, fill the little games like that. But we thought there were a lot of fun. I can remember one of my earliest regulations was feeling glad and sad about my brother Vasil because he was assigned to the CCC camp in town. My older brother, Vervil, had joined the CCCs and they had sent him to New Mexico and he got out of this little town. So my younger brother Vasil joined thinking he was going to be able to go somewhere but he was assigned right there in the hometown. I was kind of glad about Vervil Bikki. He was always kind of my favorite. My other brother, one of them, Jay and I were very close when I was a kid. He was about I think seven or eight years older than I am. He was kind of a leader of his age group and he always took me along on everything. And because he was kind of the leader, if there was going to be a game, the guys either included me or they didn't play the game. So I got to play baseball with older kids all the time. I got to play different games with the older kids. I didn't really play that much with kids my own age. I remember one particular night, Jay had several of his friends over and he was telling them about my athletic prowess and how he was teaching me to box and he told me show him your left hand, John and I hit him in the nose and brought a few tears to his eyes. His friends thought that was great. We kind of lived on the outskirts of town and we had fairly large lots. So we had a Kippa cow and we always kept chickens. And maybe we wouldn't have money enough to go to the store and buy food for ourselves. But my mom always made sure that the cows and the chickens were fed. Now, Johnny, we raised our own chicken feed and part of our cow feed. My mom always bought a hundred pound sacks of our brand and kept it by the back porch and the cow got a bucket full of it every day. But I can remember my mom going out with a pot and dipping into that old brand, picking out a few weeds that she could find and going in and running water in it and setting it on a stove and cooking that. And that was our breakfast cereal, which I really liked. Probably was the most healthy food in the world for us. The cereal that was not eaten as it congealed, I guess you would say. The next morning she would slice up and fry it in a skillet and we got that along with a large platter of fried eggs for breakfast every morning. She used to make her own syrup. We ate a lot of pancakes. She also grew some sugar cane in the garden. Not an awful lot, but she was able to get a lot of the sugar from that that she made the syrup out of. We sold eggs to neighbors. We sold milk to neighbors. We sold butter to neighbors. That was one of my jobs too, was making the butter. Once she would have a bit of sour cream, I'd put that in a quart jar, tighten the lid good and stand and shake it until butter would form in a ball. And then I would take the butter out, put it in a dish, and sprinkle a little salt in the remaining butter, making it real good buttermilk. That's where I'd got my taste for buttermilk. Really enjoyed that. The butter then had to be salted and stirred real good and she would have a mold that she would put it in to shake it. We sold that to neighbors. I also delivered quart jars of milk to neighbors. We didn't have any refrigeration, but mom would set it out on a screen in back porch in lard cans and then cover them with damp cheese cloth. Pour the milk in and set it out to the next morning you'd go out there and about an inch down would be all real thick cream. In a very young age I learned to drink a little coffee that was kind of a special treat. She would have mom would let me have a cup of coffee and I'd go out and put a spoonful of that big rich cream in it and a little spoonful of sugar and then dunk my biscuits in it. That was good. Later, mom was able to get her. No second hand ice box in which every, about every two days the ice man would come around with his horse and wagon and delivered 25 pounds of ice, put it in the ice box. The kids loved this because boy when we saw the ice wagon coming we'd all run out there and he'd take the ice pick and shave little shavings off of our previous kids to have. It was re-great. Another of my jobs was to empty the drip pan because you had, there was no place for this melting ice water to go except down through a tube and underneath the ice box so that you'd put a big square pan down there and about once or twice a day I would have to pull that up and very carefully carry it outside and dump it. As we didn't have running water in the house we had a sort of a big screened in porch off the back of the house that was just had a dirt floor. It had a pump for a wall in it in which you'd drop a rope with a bucket on the end of it down and till it heard its flourish and then you'd pull the rope back up, you had a winding mechanism around the rope up and many times I wouldn't get it hooked properly and down and it'd go again as soon as I let go of the handle. You'd have to pull it back up again. The water as I remember was excellent water. We didn't get an awful lot of rain and when it didn't rain during a week I then went on my job was to pull the rope out of it up and carry a bucket out with a dipper and pull it on the plants in the garden to make sure the day got a little moisture. Another job was always mind to pick potato bugs. I don't know where all those things come from but by the way you sure as you plant a potato there would be potato bugs all over it. They looked somewhat like a ladybug only with black and white spots on it. They didn't bite or anything but they were just irritating. Mom pretty much can. Everything that come out of the garden, any turn-ups or anything or potatoes, onions, things like that, we had a seller which they called a storm seller but it was actually for storing food also. She would can everything that she could and red shoves down there and she would store all of these canned goods down there. Potatoes were just put out on the floor to dry or to keep them from going bad. Onions were tied in bunches, a hung from the ceiling. Turn-ups were put down like the potatoes. The greens were cut up and cooked. Tasted like spinach. We like those. Put a little vinegar over them. Really hit the spot. My first recollection of eating lettuce was to taro up into small bite-sized pieces, put it in a bowl, pour some cream, sprinkle a little bit of sugar over it and eat it like cereal. I had a cousin named Carl Copenhberger who lived on a farm right on the North Canadian River, north and a little east of Gary. Once a year he would come in with his old rattle trap, flatbed truck and put out family and my aunt loves family and all the wash-tubs and buckets and bags and everything we could carry and we would go out to his farm for the day. All morning the kids had to go out and pick wild plums and grapes and you had to work hard. You couldn't mess around. You had to really pick a lot of stuff. At noon we would stop, have a big picnic lunch and in the afternoon was kind of your own. You could go in fishing or river or swim there. Whatever we had to be very careful because there was lots of quicksand in the area. So the adults would generally check things out pretty good before they would let us go near the river. But we were pretty much on our own. Got muskete a bit real bad. Just had a great day though. We always look forward to that day. When we come back for the next week or so, nothing but the smell of jell-a-e and plums cooking and grapes being squashed and cooked and all kinds of jell-a-e being canned and eating it was great. I loved the smell of it. Carl Colton-Botter's house, farmhouse, was a two-story house and it was about 200 feet from the edge of the Canadian river kind of up in Hill. But several times during the rainy season, the Canadian would flood and twice he had to be rescued out of the second-story window. The second time they had to knock him out and drag him out to win on putting him in a boat because he was refusing to leave. But he had several narrow escapes. Great life. I used to spend a tremendous amount of time at my sister Anna's place like I said about a block I have away. Her husband Cecil was sectioned for the Rock Island Railroad. He also sold fruit trees and berry bushes, grapebines, stuff like this for I believe it was a big ol' catalog. He used to send him samples of everything. So he had a large area about probably a-k-1 1,2-2 acres and so my sister always had a huge garden. She had all these fruit trees and grapebines and just everything planted all over the place. My sister grew up tremendously big garden and she was like my mother, canned everything. They also had a cow and chickens. I remember my sister used to raise these little cherry tomatoes and my nephew Melvin and I used to go out in the road in front of their place which was probably 10 to 15 feet below their level of their house. And we would crawl down the ditch with a salt shaker up and over the bank and down through the rows of tomatoes and anything that even looked like it was close to getting right. Boy we'd pick those things and sprinkle a little salt and eat them. My sister used to comment that never seemed to get any right cherry tomatoes. They would start to turn but they never got right. I wonder why. In those days just about- there wasn't hardly anybody that had indoors plumbing. So like our house was about four rooms in a path and thank God for shoes and catalogues because that was the only paper that was available to us. You could also shop at the same time. Sometimes you felt like you needed a clothespin but otherwise it wouldn't too bad except from Halloween when all the local kids used to run around trying to tip everything over. The people in the town were I guess pretty good to my mom realizing the situation she was in. I can remember one farmer used to come in every year when it was time to put in the garden and he would bring his team of course and his plows and heros and so forth and plow mom's garden up for. I'm not sure if you had a charger or anything or not. It was really very good. We got lots of hand-me-down stuff. I don't take our war or anything new in a way of clothes until probably I was in a second or third grade. Most of my stuff was hand saved from one of my brothers' work until I fitted her mom would cut it down. I don't know when she found the time but she seemed to. When I was born at home the doctor that delivered me was named John Wesley Browning and my mom named me after the doctor. My doctor gave my dad $5 for naming me after him and my dad went out to the local foregrounds to the sale and he was able to buy two calves with this $5. My sister Ruby and her husband Julyas lived on a farm outside of Gary and they took the calves out there and put them on the farm and the deal was that he was to get every third calf. After they grew up and held calves away every third calf was to be his. He was unfortunate. It seemed like for several years only the third calf lived. One time I think I heard about seven head of cattle. My mom sold them when she had an opportunity to make a down payment on her house. They were in Gary and made the down payment with it. I think she paid $600 for the house. This only made a smile on the down payment. I didn't remember this house being particularly smile but here about a year or so ago mom and I drove back to see my sisters in Oklahoma and I drove down through Gary and went down to see the place and Bobby says, my God did you live in that and it was a little dinky place with a little shed porch off the back and I remember where I slept. Like I said I didn't remember it as being a particularly smile at that time. When I saw it now I couldn't hardly believe we lived in that. In the winter the heat for the house was a big old wood burning or a coal burning stove right in the middle of the living room. I can remember that was one of my jobs so bring in every night a bucket of coal and we had a stand out and back that they would get a barrel of coal oil or caressing and it had a spigot on it and I would have to take the glass tank off of the, we had a caressing stove in the kitchen by this time and I would take this tank off and go up and fill it and put it back on the caressing stove so that there would be coal for heating and caressing for cooking. I can remember times when that stove would turn almost fiery red it would be so hot but it sure did run the house up and then mom would bank it at night before we went to bed and she'd always heat a pad around the stove before she put me to bed and then winters and put that down by my feet so that when I got the bed there was a nice warm spot. I really appreciated it. I said I didn't think I knew if I missed anything by my father dying when I was three but I can remember particularly I spent a lot of time down at Annard and Cecil's house and Cecil coming home in the afternoon would always save a little crust of bread or a little bite of something for his kids from his lunchpale and they used to just wait on pins and needles to see what he would have for them and it used to really hurt me to stand there and see this and I sure wish that I had the same thing but once in a while Cecil would make it a point to bring a little something extra and give it to me. I'm glad I love that man. As you probably remember when you were growing up I could not stand the sound of somebody chewing something crunchy like ice or crackers or something like that. It just used to drive me nuts and I think where that came from. When I was a kid I as long as I can remember I heard somebody say that it wasn't polite to chew your mouth open and I was from the time I can remember I always made a specific thing that I would chew with my mouth closed and I would be down at Annard's place when the kids would have breakfast. Oh man the smack and the loops and the chomp and the breakfast your cold breakfast, Cecil in the morning used to just absolutely drive me nuts I just couldn't really stand it. I think that's probably where my feeling came from. Food when I was a kid as I mentioned was a little on the scarce side but we always seemed to have plenty of food, meat. My brothers did a lot of rabbit hunting, squirrel hunting. I know your sister Tracy would have a fit like that but we used to eat little squirrels and once in a while about once a year my mom would get the cow bread and we'd have a calf and she'd raise that and then had it slaughtered and we'd have the meat from that and once in a while she'd get a little pig from some farmer or something and we'd raise that in a pin and then slaughter it. So we had the up to local meat market you could put meat and a lapper up there and she used to do that so that we would be able to have some fresh meat. I can remember particularly one particular thing that was so good was she would cook a slice of ham or fry it in the skillet. And it left some of the grease or something and she would put a little bit of milk and stir a lot around and it would make a cream gravy. Oh, I would almost kill for that. That was so good. Put a slice of bread on your plate and put some of that cream gravy over it. Oh, it was delicious. Most everything we had was probably fried. There wasn't much of any other way to cook stuff is at least that mom knew about. We always had a lot of potatoes to eat vegetables. We didn't hurt. I mentioned the breakfast cereals. We always had it every morning. There was a big platter of fried eggs set on the table. You can have as many as you want. There goes my cholesterol. Grady, I made a great gravy and your potatoes and gravy and if you're lucky you'd have a little meat or something. And we had family get together so they always made homemade ice cream. It took an awful lot longer than what it does today in these mixtures to make it. But boy, I tell you, talking about good ice cream, it was because mom used a lot of cream from the milk to put in there. Oh, boy, was it good? Even though it was hard work. Also this cream, anything that was left out particularly during the second world war, we sold almost all the cream. It went up to the local creamry and you would get paid by how much factor was in the cream because they used it to make explosives. You didn't dump any grease out and throw it away at that time. Everybody saved all the grease and you could go out and turn it in. My first recollection of school, our schools, not very big. There was no grass in the playground at all. We did have one huge slide. As I remember, it was probably about maybe seven feet high. Had some bars to play on and a few swings. But mainly you just got out and ran, played ball and whatever. My first day in the first grade, the teacher built the class up into three groups. The first row on the right-hand side was the Redbirds. And these are the kids that probably couldn't read very good early. I learned to read real early. I don't know how, but I did. I don't remember anybody particularly teaching me, but I suspected my sister, Riva, had a lot to do with it. When I could already read when I got in the first grade. And the first day she set it up, I was in the Redbird group because she didn't know if I could read. Then in the center of the class was the Bluebirds. Now, these were little sharper kids that didn't need quite as much attention. And then on the left-hand side, the row was the Grace Grills. And of course it was everybody's ultimate goal to reach the Grace Grills. Well, as soon as the teacher had me stand and read and saw that I could, and I guess I could read pretty good, she had me exchanged with some little girl in the Grace Grills. And I went over and sat down in that seat, and it so happens that little Suzy Q had wetter pants. And I sat quite a wet puddle. It was very embarrassing. School, particularly at that time, I don't remember all that much about it. I do remember you had music, which didn't take. I just can't really place the chairs with a typical desk with the flat part of the desk raised up, and you had a little container under there. You could put all your books and your colors and crayons and the paste. I do remember the paste that we had to get, how good it tasted. All of us kids liked to eat the paste. Teachers had quite a time telling us to quit that. I don't remember getting particularly good grades or particularly bad grades. I never got F's. I do remember one year in the fourth grade. My teacher's name was Mini Poor. And my mom used to send me to school with a quart jar of sourdough starter. And I became the teacher's pet. I could do no wrong. And my report cards from that time were straight A's. Mini Poor really liked me. See that? I didn't have it all the way through school. When I was about, I think the second or third grade, my mom had to take a job out of the home in order to make ends meet. So that left me and my two brothers at home. And I remember that she stayed at the house where she was working at this work, this doctor browning. He had had a stroke. Was bedridden. Was unable to move or talk. So she stayed there and took all the care of him. And I can remember stopping by to see her in the mornings on the way to school. And I walk into the kitchen and her and Mrs. Browning would be eating breakfast at the table. And I'd go and just stand by mom's chair and Mrs. Browning would say, have you eaten breakfast yet? And I'd say, yes, ma'am, I haven't. She said, well, then you go out and weigh outside until we're through eating. And I used to just break my heart. But that was the way she was. I used to also come down there and mow the lawns for her to just push more. Do all the trimming around the place. So that she paid mom a little bit extra for that. She had a big field out in back that she allowed my mother to go out and plant a big garden. This hairy markly came in with these horses and plowed it up for. She made a big field of corn and beans and a few other things. And she was able to sell some of that to help make ends meet also. I'd left me and my two brothers at home. My two brothers did not get along very good. It was Jay and Jesse. Jesse was a little bit different. And I think because of that, mom showed him a little more attention than the rest of us because she felt he needed it. It irritated Jay. Jay became very, very bitter about it. And later years he became extremely bitter. Thinking he was terribly mistreated as a kid all over. I never saw any of this. Jay and I became very close and it kind of turned out that it was him and I against Jesse. Jesse was a strange kid. He kind of lived in a different world of his own. Twice when we were kids, he claimed he was kidnapped. Once he claimed he had left Anna's place and was on his way home in a stranger at cost. He made him take a pill. And we couldn't find him for a while. We finally located him in a barn right next to my sister's place, a sleep. And he said the guy had given him a pill to make him go to sleep. Everything was ever found. He also claimed he was kidnapped on his way home from the town. Ben practiced. A guy took him in a car and rode him around for a while and then let him out somewhere. That was never proven either. But I can remember my mom sleeping with that gun under a pillow for a long time because she was afraid something might happen. Playing with my two brothers like the other day didn't hurt me. I don't remember particularly how we ate or anything. I do remember having to go up to the local market and to buy peanut butter. You would tell him you wanted a pound of peanut butter and he would take a cardboard container and a big scoop and scoop a lot of a tray in the meat tray. And put it in there and weigh it on the scales and then wrap it up in paper. And that's how you get your peanut butter. I'm sure we ate a lot of peanut butter and we had jelly and stuff at home. We had canned foods. So I don't think we suffered particularly. And I'm sure that some of the neighbors probably realizing that my mother couldn't get home. Probably came in with some food also. I do remember bread being bought in homemade bread. I learned to fish real early in life. I don't remember particularly how it was. But like I said, this was a great area to grow up in because there was no worries about your kids being out. You didn't feel you had to watch them every minute. So I was pretty much on my own in the summers. And me and my nephews and a couple of other friends would take off early in the morning and we'd dig a batch of worms and then we'd take off south and north and east of Gary where these farms, about every quarter to a half mile, they'd have a big farm pond. And the overflow from one pond would run across and into another pond. And any overflow from that would go to a third. And it was about probably five or six of them out. And we would start at the first one in fish and then we'd go to the second one in fish and third and fourth and fifth. And in late afternoon we would reverse the process and come back into town. We catch a few catfish, a few perch. These are went into pop for food. You didn't want to waste anything. After Dr. Browning died, my mom took a job in the local bakery. She worked six days a week. On her seventh day off she went and cleaned house and did laundry for the owner of the bakery. They treated her pretty nice. I loved it because she used to get some day old bakery goods to bring home and my favorite was a molasses cookie. Oh, those were wonderful. Really liked them. I also did the lawns for the bakery owner. My first job in the mornings before going to school was to go out and feed the chickens and then take the cow and about a twenty foot chain and a steel rod with a hammer and walk down one of the country roads and find a spot where the grass was growing and drive the stake in the ground and loop the chain over the stake and lead the cow there. Then at noon I would come home from school and move the stake a little further because the cow would have eaten a twenty foot circle around that stake and move it and then go back to school. Many afternoons when I came home I go down and I bring the cow in, pump or draw a rod or throw the cow put it on the worst tub and then put it in the pan and throw some feed in for and feed the chickens again. Gather the eggs. Mom did the milking though. I never did the milking. I was about six or seven I guess. I was old enough to go out into cotton fields and work and I couldn't afford to buy a cotton sack which they come about eight foot long or probably twenty inches wide with a shoulder strap. When you throw them out behind you and as you pick the cotton you drop it down in the sack and then shake it towards the back until you fill the sack then you pick it up throw it over your shoulder and carry it up through the field where the farmer would have a rag and with a scale and he would either be there or somebody that he designated would be there. You'd hang your cotton sack on the scale and then the guy would write down your weight and then you throw it up in the wagon and get up and shake all the cotton out of the way the sack. The way we called it snapping cotton, picking cotton was to pick the cotton out of the bowl. Snapping was picking the bowl and that's the way we mostly did it. We usually paid about a dollar and a half for a hundred pounds and when I would pick I would straddle one row on my knees and then I'd pick the row on either side of me so I'd pick three rows at once and I could pick or snap about oh I think the most I ever did was about 250 pounds in a day but I used to regularly average about 200 and was really nice at the end of the week to take four or five dollars or so or whatever I'd made to my mom and give it to her and I would usually stay out of school to do this. Sometimes they would shut down the school if the farmers needed a lot of help they would shut the school down for a week or two so that the kids could all go work in the cotton fields. I really enjoyed snapping cotton. My mom when she took in the laundry from the CCC camp would always search the pockets of course and she had a whole drawer full of different kinds of tobacco cigars, corn cob pipes, what have you and one particular time I took four pipes and a couple of packs of the tobacco when we went to the cotton fields and I got my three buddies and I we snapped enough cotton to to provide a nice mattress and we all sacked out on the mattress out in the field. We packed those pipes and lit them and we were really enjoying ourselves. This tobacco was probably, oh I don't know how many years old and dried up and really amassed but I let a sudden one of the guys said I'll be back in a minute and he got up to walk away and he fell right over and we're dealing with laughing at him like crazy and all of a sudden it hit me and I tried to get up I couldn't even get up off the sack and the next thing you know the other two were down and you have never been sick until you've been really sick from smoking and at quittentime though he afternoon the farmer came out to pick us up and we didn't even want to get on the wagon to go back into town. He had to practically drag us in and throw us up on the wagon to get us to go home and I got home I told my mom I was pretty sick I don't know it must have been something I ate but I imagine she suspected because the smell of tobacco was on us pretty strong. She never said anything though she just doctored me. I also took a job called chopping cotton and which when the cotton would first come up and get about four inches high you'd have to go through the field with a hole and chop all the weeds out and also thin it because it was planted, run right next to each other and you had to thin it some and I became quite adept at that. One time you carry a file in your pocket to keep your whole sharp and I was just a little bit yet and I'm filing my hole and my hand slipped and I cut my finger clear to the knuckle and I didn't know what to do so I just picked up a handful of dirt packed on it and then I went ahead working and at noon the farmer came around to see how we were doing and he saw my hand and he says my god what happened to you and I saw I cut it and I was sharpening my hole so he said well you don't put dirt on it and he took me over and got some router and he washed it all off and he had some tape and he taped it all up for me and to wonder I didn't get some kind of poisoning and diet but I didn't. I know a job or a farmer stopped by one day when I was out in my yard and asked me if I wanted a job and I said sure and it was kind of a drop time and he had a huge area planted with tomatoes and there was a stream or probably three or four hundred feet from the field and so my job I had a like a yolk that I put across my shoulders with a bucket on each air rope on each end with a hook on it and I would go down and fill these buckets at the stream and lay it across my shoulder and then walk out through the tomato field and with a dipper and router each plant this one on for several days it was pretty tiring but it really wasn't bad either. One of my friends when I was all probably about seven years old his father had planted a huge field of router mill and he says well we can go out there and pick one. He says just so nobody sees us. Well we each shoved a couple of potatoes in our pocket and we went out by the field there was a canyon off to the side we went down in there and gathered some twigs and leaves and such and started a little fire and we were very careful to clean up an area off around us and we burned it until we had quite a pile of coals and then we went out took some mud and packed them around the potatoes and pushed the fire aside a little bit and buried the potatoes right by it and pulled the fire back over the top and then we crawled up the side of the canyon and out into the field found a couple of router mills and rolled them ahead of us back down into the canyon and broke them open and brought us up there and scooped the hull out. Oh man was that delicious. And after a couple hours of plait around the canyon and everything we came back and we pushed the fire aside or the ashes aside and dug the potatoes up, broke the dirt off of them and all the boy they were delicious. I don't know why they were we didn't have any butter or anything to put on them but we ate them they were great. Oklahoma was a segregated state when I was a kid. I never really thought about it.
